communicative_purpose: Used to say goodbye to someone you expect to see again within a few hours
trigger_situations: Ending a conversation when you expect to see the person again soon
contexts: Daily interactions, parting from friends, colleagues, or acquaintances
common_substitutions: à plus tard, à bientôt, à tout de suite
variations: à tout à l'heure, à plus tard, à bientôt
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'au revoir' which is more permanent; using it for people you won't see for days
similar_contrasting: au revoir (more formal/final), à bientôt (less time specific), à demain (next day)
interference_warnings: English 'see you later' carries same temporal meaning; no major interference.
nuance: Casual and warm; implies a short separation period (hours, not days).
pragmatic_effect: Establishes continuity of relationship and implies upcoming reunion.
upgrade_path: à très bientôt (more formal), à tout de suite (more urgent)
chunk_family: à bientôt, à demain, à plus tard
example_1: À tout à l'heure, je te rappelle cet après-midi!
example_2: Avant de partir, elle a dit à tout à l'heure à ses collègues.
example_3: On se voit à tout à l'heure après le travail.
example_1_translation: See you later, I'll call you this afternoon!
example_2_translation: Before leaving, she said see you later to her colleagues.
example_3_translation: We'll see each other later after work.

communicative_purpose: Formal way to ask someone's health and well-being, typically used with strangers, elders, or in professional settings
trigger_situations: Greeting someone formally, beginning a conversation with someone you don't know well
contexts: Professional settings, shops, offices, formal introductions
substitution_slots: Verb conjugation varies with subject (tu/on/ils), but 'vous' form is formal default.
typical_collocates: je vais bien merci, et vous, bonjour
common_substitutions: comment allez-vous bien, comment ça va (informal), comment tu vas
variations: comment allez-vous, comment va-t-on, comment vous portez-vous
common_mistakes: Using informal 'tu' form in formal contexts; responding only with 'oui' instead of a proper reply
similar_contrasting: comment ça va (informal), comment tu vas (casual), comment vous portez-vous (very formal)
interference_warnings: English speakers may not distinguish formal vous vs informal tu; may skip 'how are you' entirely.
nuance: Professional and respectful; opens dialogue with courtesy.
pragmatic_effect: Signals politeness and acknowledgement of social distance or status.
upgrade_path: comment vous portez-vous (more formal), comment allez-vous vraiment
chunk_family: comment ça va, comment tu vas, comment allez-vous bien
example_2: En entrant dans le bureau, il a demandé comment allez-vous?
example_3: Comment allez-vous? Je vais bien, merci.
example_1_translation: Hello madam, how are you today?
example_2_translation: Upon entering the office, he asked how are you?
example_3_translation: How are you? I'm fine, thank you.

communicative_purpose: Honest admission that you lack knowledge or information about something
trigger_situations: When you genuinely don't know the answer or information; honest acknowledgment
substitution_slots: Complement optional (je ne sais pas quoi, je ne sais pas pourquoi).
typical_collocates: je ne sais pas vraiment, je ne sais pas encore, je ne sais pas trop
common_substitutions: j'en ai aucune idée, aucune idée, j'sais pas
variations: je ne sais pas, j'sais pas (informal), je ne sais point (formal/literary)
common_mistakes: Dropping 'ne' in spoken French (should be formal but common); confusing with 'je ne comprends pas'
similar_contrasting: je ne comprends pas (understanding vs knowing), aucune idée (no idea)
interference_warnings: English 'I don't know' may omit 'ne' in French speech; learners often drop negation particle.
nuance: Neutral; can be honest, evasive, or indifferent depending on tone.
pragmatic_effect: Admits limitation; shifts responsibility or conversation.
upgrade_path: je n'en ai aucune idée (stronger emphasis), je ne suis pas sûr
chunk_family: je ne comprends pas, aucune idée, j'en sais rien
example_1: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce weekend? Je ne sais pas encore.
example_2: Il a demandé la date, mais je ne sais pas.
example_3: Je ne sais pas si je vais venir demain.
example_1_translation: What are you doing this weekend? I don't know yet.
example_2_translation: He asked the date, but I don't know.
example_3_translation: I don't know if I'll come tomorrow.

communicative_purpose: To indicate the existence or presence of something
trigger_situations: Pointing out something exists, describing a location, stating facts
contexts: Describing surroundings, giving directions, stating facts
substitution_slots: Noun phrase follows (singular/plural); time expression (il y a + duration = ago).
typical_collocates: il y a beaucoup, il y a un, il y a des, il y a combien
variations: il y a, il n'y a pas, il y a eu, y a-t-il
common_mistakes: Forgetting the 'il' subject; confusing word order in questions (y a-t-il); using for time vs existence.
similar_contrasting: voilà (specific presentation), c'est (identity vs existence), il existe (formal existence)
interference_warnings: English 'there is' can omit subject; French requires 'il y a' structure.
nuance: Neutral and universal; fundamental to French sentence construction.
pragmatic_effect: Establishes fact or draws attention to something.
upgrade_path: il y a d'ailleurs (furthermore), il existe un fait que
example_1: Il y a une table dans le salon.
example_3: Il y a trois jours, on s'est rencontrés.
example_1_translation: There is a table in the living room.

communicative_purpose: To express that something is required or necessary
trigger_situations: When you need something, want to express necessity, shopping, asking for help
contexts: Daily needs, shopping, requesting assistance, explaining requirements
substitution_slots: Object varies (j'ai besoin d'eau, d'aide, de temps).
typical_collocates: j'ai besoin de, tu as besoin de, nous avons besoin de
variations: avoir besoin de, avoir grand besoin de, avoir bien besoin de
common_mistakes: Forgetting 'de' before the object; confusing with 'devoir' which means must/should
similar_contrasting: devoir (obligation vs need), avoir envie de (want vs need)
interference_warnings: English 'need' maps directly; no major interference.
chunk_family: avoir envie de, devoir, avoir l'intention de
example_2: Tu as besoin de mon aide?
example_3: Nous avons besoin de temps pour décider.
example_1_translation: I need a glass of water.

communicative_purpose: Polite request formulation, used to soften demands or show courtesy
trigger_situations: Making requests, ordering in restaurants, asking for help, formal situations
contexts: Shops, restaurants, offices, formal interactions, any polite request
typical_collocates: merci, un café s'il vous plaît, pourriez-vous
common_substitutions: s'il te plaît (informal), je voudrais (polite request)
variations: s'il vous plaît, s'il te plaît (informal), s'il vous plait
common_mistakes: Using 'te' form when formality requires 'vous'; overusing in casual contexts where it's unnecessary
similar_contrasting: s'il te plaît (informal), je voudrais (softened wish)
interference_warnings: English 'please' has no formal/informal distinction; learners may overgeneralize.
nuance: Formal politeness marker; shows respect and courtesy.
pragmatic_effect: Softens requests; shows deference and social awareness.
chunk_family: s'il te plaît, je voudrais, pourriez-vous
example_3: S'il vous plaît, parlez plus lentement.

communicative_purpose: Universal greeting used throughout the day to greet people
trigger_situations: Greeting anyone at any time of day; entering a shop; passing someone
contexts: Universal, shops, streets, offices, anywhere people meet
substitution_slots: No internal substitutions; time-based variants exist.
variations: bonjour, bonsoir (evening), bonne journée (during day parting)
common_mistakes: Using 'bonsoir' in morning/afternoon; not greeting shopkeepers when entering
similar_contrasting: bonsoir (evening), bonne journée (daytime parting), bonne nuit (nighttime parting)
interference_warnings: English 'hello' lacks time sensitivity; learners may forget 'bonsoir' for evening.
nuance: Essential ritual greeting; shows respect and social awareness.
pragmatic_effect: Opens social interaction; shows presence and respect.
example_2: En entrant dans le magasin, elle a dit bonjour.
example_2_translation: Upon entering the store, she said hello.

communicative_purpose: Stronger expression of thanks than simple 'merci', shows sincere appreciation
trigger_situations: Receiving help, gifts, favors; after someone does something kind
contexts: After receiving help, in shops, at home, anywhere gratitude is due
typical_collocates: merci beaucoup, c'est très gentil, je vous remercie
common_substitutions: merci infiniment, merci bien, merci énormément
variations: merci beaucoup, merci infiniment, merci bien
common_mistakes: Overusing in casual contexts where simple 'merci' suffices; response to 'merci' is often 'de rien'
similar_contrasting: merci (simple thanks), merci infiniment (strongest), pas de quoi (polite response)
interference_warnings: English 'thank you very much' is direct parallel; no interference.
nuance: More emphatic than 'merci'; shows genuine appreciation.
pragmatic_effect: Expresses gratitude warmly and reinforces relationship.
upgrade_path: merci infiniment, je vous suis très reconnaissant
chunk_family: merci, de rien, pas de quoi
example_2: Elle a offert un cadeau, et il a dit merci beaucoup.
example_1_translation: Thank you very much for your help!
example_2_translation: She gave a gift, and he said thank you very much.
example_3_translation: Thank you very much, that's very kind.

communicative_purpose: Getting attention before asking something, or apologizing for a minor offense
trigger_situations: Getting past someone, asking for directions, interrupting, apologizing
contexts: Streets, shops, offices, public places, any situation requiring politeness
substitution_slots: No internal substitutions; formal form only.
common_mistakes: Using 'pardon' when you mean 'excuse me' for interruption; pronouncing incorrectly
interference_warnings: English 'excuse me' overlaps; learners may confuse with apology verb.
nuance: Polite interruption or minor apology; less formal than 'pardonnez-moi'.
pragmatic_effect: Requests attention while showing courtesy and awareness of intrusion.
example_2: Elle a dit excusez-moi avant de passer.
example_3: Excusez-moi, je ne vous avais pas vu.
example_1_translation: Excuse me, where is the train station?
example_2_translation: She said excuse me before passing.
example_3_translation: Excuse me, I didn't see you.

communicative_purpose: Softer, more polite way to express a wish or request than 'je veux'
trigger_situations: Ordering in restaurants, making requests, shopping, asking for something
contexts: Restaurants, shops, hotels, anywhere making polite requests
substitution_slots: Noun phrase or infinitive follows (un café, commander, savoir).
typical_collocates: je voudrais un, je voudrais commander, je voudrais savoir
variations: je voudrais, nous voudrions, je voudrais bien
common_mistakes: Using too formally in casual settings; confusing with 'je veux' (too direct)
interference_warnings: English 'I would like' is direct parallel; may underuse in speaking.
nuance: Polite and softened; shows respect and consideration.
example_1: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.
example_1_translation: I would like a coffee, please.
example_2_translation: I would like to order something.
example_3_translation: I would like to know the time.

meaning: what is it / what is that
communicative_purpose: Asking for explanation or definition of something unknown
trigger_situations: Pointing at something unfamiliar, asking for word meanings, seeking clarification
contexts: Learning, pointing at objects, asking for explanations
substitution_slots: Demonstrative object can vary (c'est, ça, cela).
typical_collocates: qu'est-ce que c'est que ça, qu'est-ce que c'est cet objet
common_substitutions: c'est quoi (informal), qu'est-ce que c'est que ça
variations: qu'est-ce que c'est, c'est quoi ça (informal), qu'est-ce que c'est que ça
common_mistakes: Inverting word order incorrectly; forgetting the cedilla on 'ç' in 'ça'
similar_contrasting: c'est quoi (very informal), qu'est-ce que c'est que ça (emphatic)
interference_warnings: English 'what is it' may not capture the question structure; learners struggle with inversion.
nuance: Formal question structure; shows genuine curiosity.
pragmatic_effect: Requests definition or clarification in learning context.
upgrade_path: qu'est-ce que c'est exactement, pouvez-vous me l'expliquer
chunk_family: c'est quoi, qu'est-ce que ça signifie, comment dit-on
example_1: Qu'est-ce que c'est? C'est un stylo.
example_2: En montrant l'objet, elle a demandé qu'est-ce que c'est.
example_3: Qu'est-ce que c'est que ce mot?
example_1_translation: What is it? It's a pen.
example_2_translation: Pointing at the object, she asked what is it.

meaning: my name is / I call myself
communicative_purpose: Introducing yourself by stating your name
trigger_situations: Introducing yourself, filling forms, first meetings, phone calls
contexts: Introductions, formal meetings, social events, phone conversations
substitution_slots: Name or pronoun substitutes (Marc, Sophie, etc.).
typical_collocates: je m'appelle, comment vous appelez-vous, mon nom est
common_substitutions: je suis (+ profession), mon nom est (more formal)
variations: je m'appelle, je suis appelé(e), mon nom est
common_mistakes: Confusing reflexive form; using 'je suis' followed by name (means 'I am' profession/nationality)
similar_contrasting: je suis (+ profession), mon nom est (formal alternative)
interference_warnings: English 'my name is' uses different structure; learners may confuse with 'je suis'.
pragmatic_effect: Opens personal interaction and establishes identity.
upgrade_path: je m'appelle... et vous? (reciprocal), mon nom est... (formal)
chunk_family: comment vous appelez-vous, mon nom est, je suis
example_2: En arrivant, il a dit je m'appelle Antoine.
example_2_translation: Upon arriving, he said my name is Antoine.
example_3_translation: My name is Sophie, and you?

communicative_purpose: Asking about the price of something
trigger_situations: Shopping, restaurants, markets, anywhere prices are discussed
contexts: Shops, markets, cafes, street vendors, any commercial transaction
substitution_slots: No internal substitutions; time duration can precede (Ça fait combien pour une heure?).
typical_collocates: ça fait combien, c'est combien, ça coûte
common_substitutions: combien ça coûte, c'est combien, quel prix
variations: ça fait combien, c'est combien, combien ça coûte
common_mistakes: Using 'combien' incorrectly with verbs; confusing with 'combien de temps' (how long)
similar_contrasting: combien ça coûte (more common), c'est combien (colloquial)
interference_warnings: English 'how much' maps directly; no major interference.
nuance: Casual transaction speech; direct and practical.
pragmatic_effect: Opens price negotiation in commercial context.
upgrade_path: combien ça coûte exactement, quel est le prix
chunk_family: combien ça coûte, c'est combien, quel prix
example_1: Ça fait combien pour deux kilos?
example_2: Au marché, elle a demandé ça fait combien.
example_2_translation: At the market, she asked how much.

communicative_purpose: Expressing lack of understanding, asking for clarification or repetition
trigger_situations: When something is unclear, in classes, during explanations
contexts: Classes, conversations with non-native speakers, confusing situations
substitution_slots: Complement optional (je ne comprends pas pourquoi, je ne comprends pas ce mot).
typical_collocates: je ne comprends pas, pouvez-vous répéter, lentement
common_substitutions: j'ai pas compris (informal), je comprends rien (dialect), je ne saisis pas
variations: je ne comprends pas, j'ai pas compris (informal), je comprends rien
common_mistakes: Dropping 'ne' in speech; confusing with 'je ne sais pas' (not knowing vs not understanding)
similar_contrasting: je ne sais pas (knowing vs understanding), je ne saisis pas (more formal)
interference_warnings: English 'I don't understand' may omit 'ne'; French requires negation particle.
nuance: Admission of confusion; requests help or repetition.
pragmatic_effect: Signals need for clarification or slower speech.
upgrade_path: je ne comprends vraiment pas, pourriez-vous expliquer différemment
chunk_family: je ne sais pas, pouvez-vous répéter, plus lentement
example_1: Je ne comprends pas cette explication.
example_2: Pendant le cours, il a dit je ne comprends pas.
example_3: Je ne comprends pas, pouvez-vous répéter?
example_2_translation: During class, he said I don't understand.
example_3_translation: I don't understand, can you repeat?

communicative_purpose: Asking whether someone can speak French, useful when traveling or working
trigger_situations: Meeting non-French speakers, traveling, asking about language abilities
contexts: Travel, international events, hotels, airports, offices
typical_collocates: parlez-vous français, un peu, oui non, comment
common_substitutions: est-ce que vous parlez français, vous parlez français
variations: parlez-vous français, est-ce que vous parlez français, vous parlez français
common_mistakes: Wrong verb form (should use 'parlez' not 'parles'); incorrect question formation
similar_contrasting: est-ce que vous parlez français (alternative question form), vous parlez français (statement form)
interference_warnings: English word order (verb-subject inversion) is unfamiliar to learners.
nuance: Formal question; shows linguistic awareness and courtesy.
pragmatic_effect: Opens communication in foreign language context.
upgrade_path: parlez-vous francais couramment, à quel niveau parlez-vous français
chunk_family: est-ce que vous parlez français, vous parlez français, quelle langue parlez-vous
example_2: À l'aéroport, il a demandé parlez-vous français.
example_1_translation: Do you speak French? Yes, a little.
example_2_translation: At the airport, he asked do you speak French.
